risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The data manager used computer-generated randomization to allocate the 738 participants to either the intervention or nonintervention group (Fig.1)." Comment: This explicitly describes a proper random allocation method using a computer-generated sequence, which is sufficient for low risk of bias for random sequence generation.

Quote: "The data manager used computer-generated randomization to allocate the 738 participants to either the intervention or nonintervention group (Fig.1)." Comment: This describes sequence generation and who performed allocation, but it does not state any concealment mechanism (e.g., central allocation or opaque sealed envelopes) to prevent the recruiter from foreseeing the next assignment. Therefore allocation concealment cannot be judged as low risk and remains unclear.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Summary: The intervention was an educational workshop delivered to participants/caregivers and other health providers, and the paper does not report that participants or personnel were blinded. The outcomes included self-reported adherence, epilepsy beliefs/attitudes, and seizure frequency, all of which could plausibly be influenced by knowledge of allocation. Comment: Because participants and intervention deliverers necessarily knew who attended the education program, and the main outcomes are susceptible to performance/placebo/Hawthorne effects, the study is at high risk of performance bias.

Quote: "The laboratory technicians conducting the assays were blinded to the randomization. The questionnaires were administered by trained field staff, both at baseline and follow-up." Comment: Outcome assessment was mixed. The blood-level outcomes were assessed by blinded laboratory technicians, which supports low detection bias for those objective measures. However, key outcomes also included self-reported adherence and KEBAS / seizure frequency questionnaires, and these were administered by field staff rather than an explicitly blinded assessor; moreover, self-report outcomes are vulnerable because the participant is the assessor. Because the study reports subjective/patient-reported outcomes in addition to objective lab measures, the domain is judged at the level of the most vulnerable outcome and is High risk.

Quote: "Data were analyzed for 581 PWE observed at both time points." and "In the 157 PWE not seen after 1 year (because of death [45.2%], outmigration [48.4%], and withdrawal [5.7%] [Fig.1]), there were no statistically significant differences in demographic and epilepsy-related characteristics between the groups. A significantly higher proportion of those not seen after one year had more cognitive impairment, learning difficulties, and were on polytherapy compared to the 581 who were seen." Comment: Follow-up was incomplete: 157/738 were not seen at 1 year (~21.3% total loss), and missingness was not evenly distributed in prognostically important ways (those lost had more cognitive impairment, learning difficulties, and polytherapy). For the blood-level adherence outcome, only 105/303 vs 86/278 provided samples at follow-up, indicating substantial additional missing outcome data by arm. Even though the paper reports similar baseline characteristics and uses complete-case analyses, the amount and pattern of missing data are sufficient to raise a high risk of attrition bias.
